examples.title: Singly Linked List - Basic Operations
examples.description: Implementation of singly linked list with create, insert, delete, and display operations in C
examples.explanation: This C program implements fundamental singly linked list operations. The createNode function allocates memory for a new node. Insert at beginning is O(1) - just update pointers. Insert at end requires traversal O(n). Delete from beginning is O(1). Display traverses the entire list O(n).
examples.timeComplexity: Insert at beginning: O(1), Insert at end: O(n), Delete: O(1), Display: O(n)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing n nodes plus O(1) auxiliary space

examples.title: Doubly Linked List Implementation
examples.description: Python implementation of doubly linked list with insertion operations
examples.explanation: This Python code implements a doubly linked list. Each node has prev and next pointers. Insertion at beginning and end handle the case of empty list. Insert after a given node updates all four pointers correctly. The prev pointers allow backward traversal.
examples.timeComplexity: Insert at beginning: O(1), Insert at end: O(n), Insert after node: O(1)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for n nodes, O(1) auxiliary

examples.title: Linked List Reversal
examples.description: Java implementation to reverse a singly linked list using iterative approach
examples.explanation: This Java program reverses a singly linked list in-place using three pointers: prev, current, and next. The algorithm iterates through the list once, reversing each pointer. At the end, prev points to the new head. This is an in-place reversal with O(n) time and O(1) space.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) - single traversal of the list
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) - only three pointers used regardless of list size

examples.title: Floyd's Cycle Detection Algorithm
examples.description: Pseudocode for detecting cycle in linked list using two pointers
examples.code:
ALGORITHM FloydCycleDetection(head)
BEGIN
    IF head = NULL THEN
        RETURN FALSE  // Empty list has no cycle
    ENDIF
    
    slowPointer ← head      // Moves one step
    fastPointer ← head      // Moves two steps
    
    WHILE fastPointer ≠ NULL AND fastPointer.next ≠ NULL DO
        slowPointer ← slowPointer.next
        fastPointer ← fastPointer.next.next
        
        IF slowPointer = fastPointer THEN
            RETURN TRUE   // Cycle detected
        ENDIF
    ENDWHILE
    
    RETURN FALSE  // No cycle, fast reached end
END

ALGORITHM FindCycleStart(head)
BEGIN
    slowPointer ← head
    fastPointer ← head
    
    // Step 1: Detect if cycle exists
    WHILE fastPointer ≠ NULL AND fastPointer.next ≠ NULL DO
        slowPointer ← slowPointer.next
        fastPointer ← fastPointer.next.next
        IF slowPointer = fastPointer THEN
            BREAK
        ENDIF
    ENDWHILE
    
    // Step 2: Find start of cycle
    IF fastPointer = NULL OR fastPointer.next = NULL THEN
        RETURN NULL  // No cycle
    ENDIF
    
    slowPointer ← head
    WHILE slowPointer ≠ fastPointer DO
        slowPointer ← slowPointer.next
        fastPointer ← fastPointer.next
    ENDWHILE
    
    RETURN slowPointer  // Cycle start node
END
examples.explanation: Floyd's algorithm uses two pointers at different speeds. If there's a cycle, fast pointer will eventually lap around and meet slow pointer. The detection runs in O(n) time. The FindCycleStart algorithm then finds where the cycle begins by resetting one pointer to head and moving both one step at a time until they meet.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for detection, O(n) for finding cycle start
